learning_objectives: Create evenly spaced MATLAB row vectors using colon notation.
Use vector operations to evaluate many values of a formula at once.
Use vectorized complex exponentials to compute roots of an equation.
Distinguish MATLAB indexing from the independent variable of a function.
Represent and evaluate a sampled sinusoid using a time vector.

visual_plan.rationale: This section is mostly symbolic MATLAB syntax, so LaTeX-native formulas should carry the definitions. A Wikimedia complex-plane roots visual should support geometric recognition of complex roots, and a React + Canvas demo should show how a time vector samples a sinusoid. No textbook figures are available, and GPTImage2 is not needed because the important visuals are either canonical or dynamic.
visual_plan.cram: Use the visuals to instantly recognize colon-vector output, root locations on the complex plane, and MATLAB index-versus-time traps.
visual_plan.standard: Use the complex-plane reference and sinusoid sampling demo to connect one representative example to the formulas.
visual_plan.top_score: Use the visuals to notice termination-value exclusions, one-based indexing, and the difference between sample number and independent-variable value.

blocks.instruction: Create Page 1 as a minimal overview only. Use the heading 'Section Objective' followed by one sentence: 'Learn how MATLAB vectors let one expression compute many values at once.' Then add the heading 'Concepts In This Section' and list only these concept names as bullets: colon notation, vectorized complex roots, MATLAB indexing, sampled sinusoids. Do not add background paragraphs, examples, or expanded explanations on this page.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start a new page with the heading '## 1. Creating row vectors with colon notation'. Explain in 100–140 words that MATLAB's colon notation creates a row vector by starting at \(a\), repeatedly adding step size \(b\), and stopping before the next value would pass \(c\). State that \(a\) is the initial value, \(b\) is the step size, and \(c\) is the termination value. Include the representative example \(0:2:11\) producing \([0,2,4,6,8,10]\), and explicitly note that 11 is not included because the step lands on 10 and the next value would be 12. Mention that if \(b\) is omitted, MATLAB assumes a step size of 1, so \(0:11\) gives every integer from 0 through 11. Exam trigger: use this when a problem asks for evenly spaced vector entries. Common misuse: assuming the termination value always appears.

Start a new page with the heading '## 2. Vectorized complex roots'. Explain in 100–150 words that solving \(w^N=-1\) can be done by using a vector of integer values \(k=0,1,\ldots,N-1\) instead of solving one root at a time. Define \(N\) as the number of roots, \(k\) as the integer root index, and \(j\) as MATLAB's complex-axis marker; in MATLAB code, use \(1j\). Give the section's representative cube-root example: for \(N=3\), use \(k=0:2\) and \(w=\exp(1j*(\pi/3+2\pi k/3))\), producing the three roots \(0.5+0.8660i\), \(-1\), and \(0.5-0.8660i\). Exam trigger: use this when roots are evenly spaced around the unit circle. Common misuse: using too many or nonconsecutive \(k\)-values and then counting repeated roots.

blocks.search_query: Wikimedia Commons cube roots of minus one complex plane unit circle
blocks.purpose: Show the three cube roots of -1 as points evenly spaced on the unit circle in the complex plane.
blocks.fallback: skip if no accurate Wikimedia or Wikipedia roots-of-unity visual is found

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Use the image to recognize that roots are equally spaced around the unit circle.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Use the image after the formula to connect the three MATLAB outputs to their complex-plane locations.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Use the image to emphasize that different valid consecutive \(k\)-sets can list the same unique roots in a different order.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start a new page with the heading '## 3. MATLAB indexing is not function input notation'. Explain in 110–150 words that MATLAB indices are positive integers starting at 1, so \(w(5)\) means 'the fifth stored element of vector \(w\).' Use the section example: if \(k=0:99\), then the fifth element corresponds to \(k=4\), not \(k=5\). Include the check \(k(5)=4\). Explain that this is why \(f(1)\) in MATLAB means the first sample of vector \(f\), while standard math notation \(f(1)\) usually means the function value at input \(t=1\). Exam trigger: when a question asks for an element of a vector, read the number as an index unless it explicitly asks for a function value at a variable. Common misuse: treating sample index 1 as time \(t=1\).

Start a new page with the heading '## 4. Building a time vector for a sampled signal'. Explain in 90–130 words that this MATLAB command creates 500 evenly spaced time values over the interval \(0\le t<0.2\). Clarify that the step size is \(0.2/500\), so the vector starts at 0 but stops one step before 0.2. Say explicitly that subtracting \(0.2/500\) from the endpoint prevents MATLAB from including an extra endpoint sample. Representative example: the first time value is \(t(1)=0\), so any vectorized signal evaluated on this \(t\)-vector has its first sample at time zero. Exam trigger: use this pattern when a problem requests a fixed number of uniformly spaced samples on a half-open interval. Common misuse: writing \(0:0.2/500:0.2\) and accidentally creating 501 samples.

Continue the same sinusoid page after the time-vector formula. Explain in 80–120 words that MATLAB evaluates this formula at every entry of the vector \(t\) when writing \(f=\sin(2*pi*10*t+pi/6)\). Define 10 as the frequency in hertz, \(2\pi\cdot 10t\) as the angle accumulated over time, and \(\pi/6\) as the phase offset. Include the representative check: because \(t(1)=0\), the first stored value is \(f(1)=\sin(\pi/6)=0.5000\). Exam trigger: use vectorized evaluation when one formula must be computed at many sample times. Common misuse: reading MATLAB \(f(1)\) as \(f(t)\) at \(t=1\).

blocks.title: Sample a sinusoid with a MATLAB time vector
blocks.instruction: Create an interactive React + Canvas demo on the same sinusoid page. Show a clean coordinate plot of \(f(t)=\sin(2\pi\cdot 10t+\pi/6)\) over \(0\le t<0.2\). Overlay sample dots generated by the MATLAB-style vector \(t=0:T/N:T-T/N\), with default \(T=0.2\) and \(N=500\). Include controls for sample count \(N\) with presets 20, 50, 100, 500, and a toggle labeled 'show endpoint mistake' that switches to \(t=0:T/N:T\). Display a small readout for number of samples, first sample \(f(1)\), last time value, and whether the endpoint \(0.2\) is included. Keep the visual white-background, restrained, and lecture-note clean.
blocks.what_to_notice_instruction: Below the demo, write three bullets: the vector index starts at 1; the first sample is at \(t=0\), not \(t=1\); including the endpoint creates one extra sample.

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Focus on the endpoint mistake and the index-versus-time readout.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Use the sliders to connect the formula, time vector, and plotted samples.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Use low sample counts to see how sample density changes the visual representation without changing the underlying sinusoid formula.

blocks.instruction: Create the recap page titled '📌 Key Takeaways'. Include exactly 5 bullets, each ≤24 words. The bullets must explicitly include these formulas: \(a:b:c\to[a,a+b,a+2b,\ldots]\); \(w_k=e^{j(\pi/N+2\pi k/N)}\); \(f(1)_{\text{MATLAB index}}\ne f(t)|_{t=1}\); \(t=0:0.2/500:0.2-0.2/500\); and \(f(t)=\sin(2\pi\cdot10t+\pi/6)\). End with one sentence: 'Next, these vector skills make it easier to generate and inspect signals efficiently.'

knowledge_points.label: MATLAB colon notation for row vectors

questions.stem: What vector does MATLAB create from the command k = 0:2:11?
questions.explanation: The vector starts at 0 and adds 2 repeatedly. It stops at 10 because the next value, 12, would pass 11.

wrong_option_explanations.B: The termination value is included only if the step lands on it exactly.
wrong_option_explanations.C: That would be 0:11, where MATLAB assumes a step size of 1.

questions.hint: Start at 0, add 2 each time, and stop before passing 11.

questions.explanation: The values decrease from 11 by \(10/3\) each step: 11, 7.6667, 4.3333, 1.0000.

wrong_option_explanations.B: A positive step cannot move from 11 down toward 0.
wrong_option_explanations.D: A step of -3 gives 11, 8, 5, 2, not the listed values.

questions.hint: The entries are decreasing, so the step must be negative.

questions.stem: To compute the three unique roots of \(w^3=-1\) using \(w=\exp(1j*(\pi/3+2\pi k/3))\), which MATLAB vector for k is appropriate?
questions.explanation: Three consecutive integer values are needed. The textbook uses k = 0:2, which gives k = 0, 1, 2.

wrong_option_explanations.B: This can produce the same unique roots in a shifted order, but it does not match the section's representative MATLAB setup.
wrong_option_explanations.C: This gives four k-values, causing a repeated root.
wrong_option_explanations.D: These are not consecutive integers and do not represent the root index correctly.

questions.hint: For three unique roots, use exactly three consecutive integer values.

questions.stem: Observe a complex-plane plot showing three roots of \(w^3=-1\). Which visual pattern should the correct roots have?
options: A. Three points equally spaced on the unit circle
B. Three points equally spaced on the real axis
C. Three points with different distances from the origin
D. Three points all in the first quadrant
questions.explanation: The roots of \(w^3=-1\) have magnitude 1 and are separated by equal angular spacing.

wrong_option_explanations.B: Only one of the listed roots is purely real.
wrong_option_explanations.C: All three roots lie on the unit circle, so they have the same magnitude.
wrong_option_explanations.D: The roots appear in different directions around the complex plane.

questions.hint: Complex exponential roots usually appear as points around a circle.

questions.stem: If k = 0:99, what is k(5) in MATLAB?
questions.explanation: MATLAB indices start at 1. The first element is 0, so the fifth element is 4.

wrong_option_explanations.A: This confuses the index number with the stored value.
wrong_option_explanations.C: 0 is the first element, not the fifth.

questions.stem: A classmate says MATLAB f(1) means the function value at t = 1. Explain why that is wrong in this section's sampled-signal example.
questions.ideal_answer: In MATLAB, f(1) means the first stored element of the vector f. In the sampled-signal example, the first time value is t(1)=0, so f(1) is f(t) evaluated at t=0, not at t=1.
grading_rubric: Must state that MATLAB f(1) is an index operation.
Must state that the first sample corresponds to t(1)=0.
Must distinguish MATLAB vector syntax from standard function notation.
questions.explanation: This checks the main indexing trap from the section.
questions.hint: Ask whether the number inside parentheses is an index or a function input.

questions.stem: For t = 0:0.2/500:0.2-0.2/500, how many time samples are intended?
questions.explanation: The interval length is divided into 500 steps, and the endpoint is excluded so the vector contains 500 samples.

wrong_option_explanations.B: That would happen if the endpoint 0.2 were included using 0:0.2/500:0.2.
wrong_option_explanations.C: The construction includes the first sample at 0 and stops one step before 0.2.
wrong_option_explanations.D: 0.2 is the interval length, not the number of samples.

questions.hint: The expression subtracts one step from the endpoint to avoid an extra sample.

questions.stem: Using \(f(t)=\sin(2\pi\cdot10t+\pi/6)\) and the time vector whose first value is \(t(1)=0\), what is MATLAB f(1)?
questions.explanation: The first sample uses t=0, so f(1)=sin(pi/6)=0.5000.

wrong_option_explanations.B: The phase offset \(\pi/6\) makes the value nonzero at t=0.
wrong_option_explanations.D: That expression corresponds to t=1, but MATLAB f(1) is the first vector element, not t=1.

questions.hint: Use the first stored time value, not the number inside f(1) as a time.
